author: @MrArcher0
text:
I truly love the work your doing guys. I just want to give everyone a brief history lesson on Asbestos and let's see if anyone else draws any parallels like I am. Asbestos is a completely inert material that is heat and chemical resistant. The world embraced these properties and put the darn stuff in everything from the plastics you use today in your pot handles, floor tile, shingles, break pads, pipe insulation, counter tops ( labs mostly), the list goes on and on.These fibers are so small and light that they take 3 days to settle out of the air in an disturbed room. Yet if they get in your lungs they form scar tissue due to their size and needle like structure. Asbestos effects on the body take decades to develop. Are you sure you want to develop a fine dispersal device to add this to concrete? 
Just stepping back and looking at the big picture...

author: @cyatlas
text:
Hi Robert, 
2 further ways amongst others to explore:
1. mix tiny glass balls inside to reduce weight and receive at a certain ratio even the possibilty to have ' floating concrete '.
2. mix optical fibers in it to receive ' translucent ' concrete. 4-5% is the standard ratio for translucency. 
Would be interesting to see how graphene is influencing this next to increase the stiffness.
Herri

replies.text:
Hi Robert

Two questions
1. Can you do a video on the recipe for your concrete? Or is it proprietary at this point. 

2. I am wondering if this carbon based concrete has mold issues. The reason why I ask this is because carbon can house mold. If that is the case that the graphene promotes mold in the concrete, can you add a mold inhibitor like borate or other mold inhibiting chemicals to the mix.
I think the chemicals that they used to make sure pallets don't rot would be a good place to start. There would be other non-toxic and or safe chemicals to inhibit mold in the concrete I think.

What is your opinion about the graphene promoting mold in the concrete?

author: @vladimirstrunga37
text: This is really excellent, congratulations! I was working on the alternative cement and geopolymere mixtures some years ago and I am still very interested in. Is there really graphene or rather graphene oxide used? My concern is about hydrophobicity / hydrophilicity of the graphitic materials. Expected hydrophilicity of graphene oxide might be an advantage in dispersing and better compatibility with C-S-H gels during hydration reactions. And what is the portion of graphene in the mixture? Is it in order of wt% or less?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it's graphene mate and in the order of 0.5% by weight or less

replies.author: @vladimirstrunga37
replies.text: This is inspiring. Two weeks ago I did an experiment with melting natural graphite from South Bohemia in NaOH (850°C, 1 hour), like for the silicate analysis. In fact, I was interested in how it will behave and whether there is a lot of silicate component in it. I did not quantify it, however, a silicate gel has formed after neutralising the alkali solution from dissolved melt. Slight exfoliation or disintegration of graphite occurred too, as revealed by a small part of a soft dark fraction that was not readily settled down to the bottom. Now I see a possible way how to use this natural graphite with silicate impurities if I can economically exfoliate it in an alkali environment. There are many abandoned graphite mines in some parts of the country. It's like the dump coal you have suggested to be used. Great!

author: @ACTlVISION
text: I don't want to ask for anything you can't divulge but what ratio of graphene (by mass?) is used in the mixture? I'm playing with some decent <10nm platelets I picked up off ebay and some good marine grade epoxy, just looking for a ballpark to start with so I don't waste as much material. Thanks!

replies.author: @ACTlVISION
replies.text:
Thanks, at that rate I have enough graphene (50g) to make over 20 pounds of reinforced epoxy! I also want to try making it thermally conductive for other uses, so I'll definitely try to see what happens when I up the percentage. 

May I also ask the best ways to mix it? I'm working on building a magnetic mixer by gluing a neodymium magnet onto a computer fan and hoping that does the trick with uniform dispersion, but I know it'll get messy with the magnetic spinning bit that has to go inside the epoxy.

replies.author: @Buzzhumma
replies.text: WHAT THE PLANTS CRAVE you want to mix it into a solvent first though to get your best attempt at dispersing !

replies.author: @Buzzhumma
replies.text: and then into both of your epoxy mixes but separately and only bring them together when ready to use . watch out for accelerated reaction as well as the graphene may make it a lot more thermally conductive!

author: @AtlasReburdened
text: I wonder if aligning the plane of the graphene plates perpendicular to the the compression vector would simulate engineered dirt and vastly improve compressive strength, or if it would just create shear prone boundaries layers.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: interesting idea - but - how would you do that in a way that can be done on a building site mate?

replies.author: @AtlasReburdened
replies.text: Kinda depends on what works in the lab. I see three options as far as I can think off the top of my head. One could use flow though a prepared medium to induce the alignment, but that would probably require concrete without aggregate, and vastly more powerful slurry pumps. I believe it is also within the realm of possibility to induce alignment through treatment of wet graphene doped concrete with high frequency or possibly just high amplitude, electric and magnetic fields, and a system that does that as far as I know could be plopped on the end of a boom lift and safely controlled from the base. The third that may work would just be a particular patterning of the tamping they already do to get air pockets out. I mean, in detail it's mostly speculation but all of those examples are based on processes that exist, just not for that application yet.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: awesome - cheers mate

replies.author: @AtlasReburdened
replies.text: Yeah, there is that, but the significance of that property to the bulk characteristics seems as though it would be largely proportional to the size of sheets being used. author: @Allwestconstruction
text:
If I read the machine correctly, that test reached 6,000 PSI in 7 days, that is amazing 

Can't wait to see  the 28 day break test 

I have worked with a lot of concrete mixes in the past and the normal spec for commercial concrete is 3,000 psi in 28 days which is required by building code

Using your mix design could be a huge saving on concrete mix as you would need less cement to reach the required PSI

Very nice

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that's about right mate but th results are given in the video on the table as MPa

replies.author: @mutantgenepool
replies.text: Oh dear. That's (6000 MPa) 870226.4 PSI

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: the graph shows the validated results mate

replies.author: @Baigle1
replies.text:
The display measures XXXX(.)X in digits. The measurement is in KN. Frame by frame the max measurement on the Impact Test CT440 (or similar because their user guides are the same) machine is 619.8KN of force. You need to know the size of the cube whether it be 100, 150, or 200mm cubed in order to take the measurement to MPa. Judging from the size vs the upper and lower 300mm platens on the machine the block looks to be 200mm* (measuring using cage spacing), and marked by the blue alignment spacers where to put the block in the machine. Southern Testing states cubes 100mm and 150mm can be tested. The 200mm guesstimate from measuring platen distances is betrayed by the 28.5MPa results, which work out that 619.8KN/22500mm(sq) as of a 150x150mm cube leads to 0.027546667 KN/mm2 which works out to 27.5MPa or 3988PSI. The footage likely didn't show the maximum load on the display as the machine recorded.

On the relationship between 7, 14, 28 day and further strength tests:
"The chart is not standard.  Say a mix has a quantity of flyash substituted for cement.  This mix might reach only 50% at 7 days and only 90% at 28 days.

Every mix cures and gains strength at different rates.  Otherwise every 7 day break for every 3000 psi mix would be exactly the same."

Southern Testing offers many different tests regarding concrete strength, including impurities and water and mineral infiltration statistics. They are key to seeing quality and how well the strength holds over time.

28.5MPa / 4133PSI on 7 day test is pretty good, what cement and water mixture did you guys use? If it can improve the quiky cheap-o stuff from the store that much it really has an opportunity!


http://www.eng-tips.com/viewthread.cfm?qid=134082

https://www.southerntesting.co.uk/services/laboratory-testing/concrete-testing/

http://www.impact-test.co.uk/products/5007-automatic-console-3000kn-cube-and-cylinder-compression-machine/

http://www.impact-test.co.uk/docs/CT340-CT770%20Automatic%20Compression%20Machines_HB.pdf

http://www.cement.org/cement-concrete-basics/products/high-strength-concrete

http://www.cement.org/learn/concrete-technology/concrete-design-production/ultra-high-performance-concrete

https://www.quikrete.com/pdfs/concreteselectorguide.pdf

https://quikrete.com/productlines/fastsetconcretemix.asp

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Types_of_concrete

replies.text: it was made according to BS206 mate from CEM A we got from the local builders merchant - it's meant to improve the quickie mix used in your average cheapo mix - so basically we took that and chucked a bit of graphene in - nothing fancy - nothing engineered no other additives or special treatment

replies.author: @Baigle1
replies.text:
if the heat doesn't destroy the structures as it makes the bonds with the iron and other additives at 1648C (2900-3000F) you might end up with a higher strength high carbon steel, but you would need to control for the size of graphene sheets in the end product and mixing.

http://poplab.stanford.edu/pdfs/PopVarshneyRoy-GrapheneThermal-MRSbull12.pdf

graphite is already used in carbonization of crucible steel production at scale. the end % of carbon in the steel is ~0.04 to 1.5% carbon with other additives and very few impurities. I take it that since graphene is ablated layer by layer with diffuse lasers at around 2000K (1727C) that most of the graphene would be burned up or reacted.

https://www.nature.com/articles/srep11546

https://www.steel.org/making-steel/how-its-made/processes/processes-info/the-basic-oxygen-steelmaking-process.aspx

the larger sizes of graphene sheets and their reactivities with other elements in the process may cause a lower quality steel to be made as either the graphene would be damaged and weakened through chemical reactions, or the sheets would more or less always get pulled into the slag or cause inclusions if intact.

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Steelmaking

TL;DR: When you are making crucible steel its too hot, large sheets or clumps cause lower quality steel, impurities and reactiveness with additives and waste slag lower quality, and they already use graphite.

P.S. The steel making article was using sources around 1998-1999 for information. Still good, but it would be cool to know what new techniques and systems are in use today.
